revision.text: Nakayama-dera is a Buddhism temple, in [[Takarazuka]], [[Hyogo]], [[Japan]]. The ‘[[engi]]’, or the official history of the temple, says that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century. It is one of the most popular temples in the city. The principal object of worship at this temple is a [[Juichimen-Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has her power to give babies and its easy delivery. Today’s buildings of this temple were mainnly built by [[Hideyori Toyotomi]] in the 17th century. Hideyori is a son of [[Hideyoshi Toyotomi]] who unified Japan in 16th century. The entrance stations to this temple are [[Nakayama Station]] of [[Hankyu Takarazuka line]] and JR [[Nakayama-dera Station]] of [[Fukuchiyama line]].

[[image:Nakayama-dera.jpg|thumb|250px]]
'''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a Buddhist temple, in [[Takarazuka]], [[Hyogo]], [[Japan]]. The ‘[[engi]]’, or the official history of the temple, says that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century. It is one of the most popular temples in the city. The principal object of worship at this temple is a [[Juichimen-Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has her power to give babies and its easy delivery. Today’s buildings of this temple were mainnly built by [[Hideyori Toyotomi]] in the 17th century. Hideyori is a son of [[Hideyoshi Toyotomi]] who unified Japan in 16th century. The entrance stations to this temple are [[Nakayama Station (Hyogo)|Nakayama Station]] of [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line]] and JR [[Nakayama-dera Station]] of [[Fukuchiyama Line]].
[[ja:中山寺]]
[[Category:Buddhist temples in Hyogo]]

[[File:Nakayama-dera.jpg|thumb|Hondo]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a Buddhist temple, in [[Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]].
The [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] No.24.
==History==
The ‘engi’, or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century. It is one of the most popular temples in the region. Today’s buildings of this temple were mainnly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori is a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of Worship==
The principal object of worship at this temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has her power to give babies and its easy delivery.
==Access==
The entrance stations to this temple are [[Nakayama Station (Hyogo)|Nakayama Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
{{coord|34|49|17.89|N|135|22|3.67|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}}
{{Commonscat|Nakayamadera, Takarazuka}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Nakayama-Dera}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} [[Category:Buddhist temples in Hyōgo Prefecture]]
[[fr:Nakayama-dera]] [[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]]

The [[Kansai Kannon Pilgrimage]] No.24.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century. It is one of the most popular temples in the region. Today's buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.

[[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20151001014158/http://www.nakayamadera.or.jp/top.html Nakayama-dera] * [http://www.kinki36fudo.org/ kinki36fudo] {{Coord|34|49|17.89|N|135|22|3.67|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}}
{{Commons category|Nakayamadera, Takarazuka}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}}

It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==

==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[Buddhist temple]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20151001014158/http://www.nakayamadera.or.jp/top.html Nakayama-dera] * [http://www.kinki36fudo.org/ kinki36fudo] {{Coord|34|49|17.89|N|135|22|3.67|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}}

{{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] in [[Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[Jūichimen Kannon]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[Hankyu Railway]] [[Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[JR West]] [[:en:Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]
== External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20151001014158/http://www.nakayamadera.or.jp/top.html Nakayama-dera] * [http://www.kinki36fudo.org/ kinki36fudo] {{Coord|34|49|17.89|N|135|22|3.67|E|region:JP_scale:20000_source:jawiki|display=title}}
{{Commons Category|Nakayamadera, Takarazuka}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Buddhist temples in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Temples of Avalokiteśvara]]
}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Nakayamadera Pagoda.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Takarazuka|qid=Q823967}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.82163611|N|135.36768611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|qid=Q11404731}}|part_of={{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|qid=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q11498552|qid=Q11498552}}; {{ill|Q131552696|qid=Q131552696}}; {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|qid=Q750721}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3335345]] Wikidata name: Nakayama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] in [[:en:Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyori|Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[:en:Nakayama-kannon Station|Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[:en:Nakayamadera Station|Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[:en:Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku|Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[sn:commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Nakayamadera Pagoda.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Takarazuka|WD=Q823967}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.82163611|N|135.36768611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q11404731}}|part_of={{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q11498552|WD=Q11498552}}; {{ill|Q131552696|WD=Q131552696}}; {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3335345]] Wikidata name: Nakayama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] in [[:en:Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyori|Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[:en:Nakayama-kannon Station|Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[:en:Nakayamadera Station|Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[:en:Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku|Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyori|Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.

The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.

It is the 24th temple in the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyori|Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[:en:Nakayama-kannon Station|Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[:en:Nakayamadera Station|Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[co:commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[co:commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[sn:commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Nakayamadera Pagoda.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182}}|prefecture={{ill|Takarazuka|WD=Q823967}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.82163611|N|135.36768611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q11404731}}|part_of={{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581}}; {{ill|Q11498552|WD=Q11498552}}; {{ill|Q131552696|WD=Q131552696}}; {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3335345]] Wikidata name: Nakayama-dera [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples in Japan]] in [[:en:Takarazuka, Hyōgo|Takarazuka]], [[:en:Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the [[:en:Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage]]. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyori|Toyotomi Hideyori]] in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Ekādaśamukha]], or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are [[:en:Nakayama-kannon Station|Nakayama-kannon Station]] on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and [[:en:Nakayamadera Station|Nakayamadera Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Fukuchiyama Line|Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)]].
==See also== *[[:en:Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku|Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori}} in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha}}, or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.

The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are {{ill|Nakayama-kannon Station|en|Nakayama-kannon Station|WD=Q3970265|lt=Nakayama-kannon Station}} on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and {{ill|Nakayamadera Station|en|Nakayamadera Station|WD=Q856035|lt=Nakayamadera Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuchiyama Line|en|Fukuchiyama Line|WD=Q1154538|lt=Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[co:commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[co:commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[arz:ناكاياما ديرا]][[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Nakayamadera, Takarazuka]][[de:Nakayama-dera]][[en:Nakayama-dera]][[fr:Nakayama-dera]][[ja:中山寺 (宝塚市)]][[zh:中山寺 (寶塚市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Nakayamadera Pagoda.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}}|prefecture={{ill|Takarazuka|WD=Q823967|1=Takarazuka, Hyōgo}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.82163611|N|135.36768611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}|part_of={{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581|1=Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground}}; {{ill|Q11498552|WD=Q11498552|1=Q11498552}}; {{ill|Q131552696|WD=Q131552696|1=Q131552696}}; {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in {{ill|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|en|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|WD=Q823967|lt=Takarazuka|1=Takarazuka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}} in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha|1=Ekādaśamukha}}, or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are {{ill|Nakayama-kannon Station|en|Nakayama-kannon Station|WD=Q3970265|lt=Nakayama-kannon Station|1=Nakayama-kannon Station}} on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and {{ill|Nakayamadera Station|en|Nakayamadera Station|WD=Q856035|lt=Nakayamadera Station|1=Nakayamadera Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuchiyama Line|en|Fukuchiyama Line|WD=Q1154538|lt=Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)|1=Fukuchiyama Line}}.
==See also== *[[:en:Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku|Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]

{{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Nakayamadera Pagoda.JPG|religious_affiliation={{ill|Shingon Buddhism|WD=Q550182|1=Shingon Buddhism}}|prefecture={{ill|Takarazuka|WD=Q823967|1=Takarazuka, Hyōgo}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.82163611|N|135.36768611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara|WD=Q11404731|1=Ekādaśamukha}}|part_of={{ill|Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground|WD=Q11638581|1=Kinki Thirty-six Fudoson Sacred Ground}}; {{ill|Q11498552|WD=Q11498552|1=Q11498552}}; {{ill|Q131552696|WD=Q131552696|1=Q131552696}}; {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}}} {{Short description|Buddhist temple in Takarazuka, Japan}} [[File:Nakayama-dera Main Hall 201608.jpg|thumb|Hondō (Main hall)]] [[File:Nakayamadera daimon02s2048.jpg|thumb|Daimon]] [[File:Nakayama-dera Five-storied Pagoda 201708.jpg|thumb|Pagoda]] '''Nakayama-dera''' (中山寺 ''Nakayama-dera'') is a {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|WD=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples in Japan|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} in {{ill|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|en|Takarazuka, Hyōgo|WD=Q823967|lt=Takarazuka|1=Takarazuka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} established in the 6th century. It is the 24th temple in the {{ill|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|en|Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|WD=Q750721|lt=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage|1=Saigoku Kannon Pilgrimage}}. It is one of the most popular temples in the region.
==History==
The ''engi'', or the official history of the temple, recorded that the temple was founded by [[:en:Prince Shotoku|Prince Shotoku]], in the 6th century.
Present-day buildings of this temple were mainly built by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|WD=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}} in the 17th century. Hideyori was a son of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} who unified Japan in the 16th century.
==Objects of worship==
The principal [[Shintai|object of worship]] (''shintai'') at the temple is a {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|WD=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha|1=Ekādaśamukha}}, or the Goddess of Mercy with eleven heads. It is believed the Kannon has the power to give babies and an easy delivery.
==Access==
The railway stations near the entrance of the temple are {{ill|Nakayama-kannon Station|en|Nakayama-kannon Station|WD=Q3970265|lt=Nakayama-kannon Station|1=Nakayama-kannon Station}} on the [[:en:Hankyu Railway|Hankyu Railway]] [[:en:Hankyu Takarazuka Line|Takarazuka Line]] and {{ill|Nakayamadera Station|en|Nakayamadera Station|WD=Q856035|lt=Nakayamadera Station|1=Nakayamadera Station}} on the {{ill|West Japan Railway Company|en|West Japan Railway Company|WD=Q502125|lt=West Japan Railway Company|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Fukuchiyama Line|en|Fukuchiyama Line|WD=Q1154538|lt=Fukuchiyama Line (JR Takarazuka Line)|1=Fukuchiyama Line}}.
==See also== *[[:en:Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku|Historical Sites of Prince Shōtoku]]
